sentences.text: My name is Henry  Binstock.
H-E-N-R-Y B-I-N-S-T-O-C-K.
Same name.
My name at birth is Herszl.
H-E-R-S-Z-L.
No.
No other names.
No.
October 9, 10th year.
11th year.
85 Years.
What year?
What city?
Losice.
L-O-S-I-C.
Losice.
Poland.
It was a small town.
It was about 7,000 Jews lived in Losice in the city.
And all the way around, there was the Catholics Christians was living as farmers.
And it was a little business in the city.
It was storekeepers-- all-- all Jewish people in the city.
And it was little shops-- tailors, shoemakers, and Orthodox Jews, rabbis.
I used to go in the temple.
Saturday was a big holiday and Friday.
And--
People-- people would come Friday and for Saturday.
You know, they-- all went together in the temple.
It was  a little religious, Jewish little city.
And there was-- it was a public school.
There were private schools for the children like cheders.
You know?
And there-- that was the life.
It was not-- there was no electricity in the beginning when I was born.
Then later, they started to build up the city with-- with electricity and used to make theaters.
It was-- they built a theater.
And the-- was organizations.
There was a Zionist organization.
And there was a organization with workers.
And they are more in the leftist side-- it was a little on the Communist side, there were poor people.
And-- and it was-- it was really a poor-- a poor little town of poor people.
But later on, they started to build up with the businesses.
And in my family, it was-- we started to make shoes for a shoe store.
And shoe stores-- we got salesmen on the road, too.
And there was-- it was one-- one big family-- three brothers.
We used to go-- we got workers.
We used to work and make a lot of shoes for-- for stores.
And this-- the salesmen used to send us the orders.
And-- this was before the war.
And then-- and we got-- we-- the businesses started getting bigger.
And we used to send for the shoes-- when the-- other towns, and the other cities.
And then when the war broke out, we-- we-- the-- the last day when the war broke out, this was in-- in September the 1st.
It was broke out the war.
And the same day, we sent away a lot of shoes-- about-- about 1,000 pair of shoes in the-- in different areas.
Well--
Yeah.
My mother was with-- they-- we-- we didn't have in that time before the war, we didn't have refrigerators.
We didn't have-- we didn't have toilets in the house.
We didn't have water in the house.
I had to go far away to-- to bring water.
And they had to make fire.
And, you know, it was not stoves to build up the ovens with bricks.
If they built up a stove, they have to make fire from wood.
And so it was a hard life with cooking-- with-- with running the house for the children-- for the household.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- the-- my father used to go-- he was a little bit religious.
He used to go every morning in the temple to pray.
And then-- and then this was the life.
It was a hard life.
And--
My father's name was Avrum-- Abraham.
My mother's name was Fejga.
My mother's name is Feja is F-E-J-G-A.
Fejga.
And Avrum was A-V-R-U-M.
Brothers?
My older brother was Berl.
And the younger brother-- my sister-- my younger brother was Jeshua.
And then I got the sisters-- Rivka, and Chaja, Esther, and Bayla, and Sarah.
That's five sisters.
No.
Well, we was-- the whole family was in the business.
The-- the whole family was working in their only business together.
No, it was home.
It was home.
And we used to give out a lot of work outside other workers.
And they used to take it in the morning.
There were-- in the evening, they'll bring it back.
Yeah.
Outside, yeah, used to-- used to be about four or five workers in our house besides us.
Well, he was working about-- about 12 people.
Yes.
Yeah.
No, she was running the house for the family.
Household, no.
It was a house.
It was a big house.
It was-- three families were living in that house.
It was three-- three separate suites.
Well, it was-- there was a kitchen.
And it was-- a bedroom, in the front room, it was together.
And then we got a special shop another room, was working-- people was working-- about-- about 10-- about 10 people, 10, 12 people was working the shop.
Well, we were sleeping in one room.
[LAUGHS]
Two sisters-- two sisters were sleeping in one-- one bed, and two brothers in a separate bed, and the rest-- the rest of the children on a different bed, and the father and the mother were separate.
No, it wasn't a separate.
[LAUGHS]
Well, my-- my job in the shop?
Yeah, I was just working making shoes.
I went to school.
I went in the public school for a couple years before I  went to a cheder.
I went to private school.
Then I went a couple years in the public school.
Oh, I got a lot of hobbies.
I would like-- I like always to fix things.
And when we got in the-- in our shop the electricity, and like anything that happens to the electricity I was fixing myself.
I was-- I liked to do things.
I was-- I--
No.
No radio, no nothing.
Yeah, I was playing in-- the instrument was a mandolin-- you hear about it.
It's like-- rounded like a  guitar.
You know?
That's what I was playing myself-- I was trying to do it.
I didn't do it for a long, long time.
Yeah.
I was-- I was singing in a group.
And I was-- and we got-- our organization was a Zionist organization.
We used to play-- we-- we played-- I got even a group in Israel where our people now in our hometown was-- is-- written a book many years ago.
And I got that book in Yiddish.
But they were writing about me.
I was in the top players.
It was all kind of these theaters we used to make with their names maybe.
But it was in theaters.
And I used to participate in the theaters.
No, I was-- I was acting.
Yeah.
I once-- I played-- I played in a   play.
It was from the Zionist organization.
If he wrote it about a big-- this officer that was in the Jewish-- in the Jewish Army-- he was-- he was-- the-- the Arabs killed him-- by the name Trumpeldor.
Have you heard about Trumpeldor?
A big officer.
He was-- he was in the English Army with-- with one hand.
He's got-- he lost an arm in the English Army in Israel.
And he was from the first-- the first kibbutzim-- first kibbutz in Israel.
And they played-- played a play about him about that time.
And I played the-- his role.
I played-- I played Trumpeldor.
Well, it was-- it was-- I belonged to a club, where they was playing often different kinds of plays.
Yeah.
[LAUGHS]
This I play-- I sing in the-- in the Jewish-- in the Jewish-- in the Jewish organization here in Cleveland.
I was singing there.
I even got a picture from them when I was singing in a group.
[LAUGHS] I don't have anything.
Hobbies?
What are--
I can dance.
I was-- I was running-- I was with another friend in Losice.
We got this dance group, was learning-- learning people dancing.
Yeah, I was a good dancer, but [LAUGHS] not now.
I can dance.
But not--
Well, I traveled away.
And one time before when my father started to launch the business with salesmen, I went to kibbutz in Bialystok in Poland.
I was three months in the kibbutz.
And I was trying to get a certificate to go to Israel at that time.
And it was-- I had to be there too long.
It was my friend who was there for three years.
And it's got a hard time to get a certificate.
And so I left the kibbutz.
I didn't give up.
I was trying to go.
But it was no way-- not-- then when the war broke out, it was-- was a waste.
My friends?
Oh, I got a lot of friends.
Not too many non-Jewish friends-- not in our city.
Well, it was-- in our city was real bad.
We didn't-- we didn't like each other.
But in the school, in the-- in the public school-- they used to go together.
You know?
But it was not too friendly, because it was the anti-Semitic in that time.
It was-- was too much, anti-Semitic there, especially the government.
It was-- the main general in Poland was his name, Pilsudski.
He was a general.
And he was protecting the Jews, because in the war when-- when Pilsudski-- Polish people took over from Russia, Poland in the Second World War-- in the First World War, I mean.
So Pilsudski didn't trust his own people.
And he used to-- when-- when they looked for him, the-- the-- the Russians at the time, the-- the Communists took over in the First World War.
So he was hiding Jews only.
He came here once to-- he was hiding once by a Jew.
And they came in to look for him.
The Jew put him in a tallis and tefillin.
[LAUGHS] And he was saying a prayer.
You know?
So-- so he was-- was protecting the Jews all these-- all these years until he died in Poland.
How did you know about this?
Well, it was-- it was the story.
They were writing in the papers about it.
Yeah, it was written in the papers.
[DRINKS] What?
When they started changing?
Well, the war broke out.
So we-- after the war was changing-- when war broke out.
It was-- the war broke out in-- in September the 1st.
In--
The war broke out.
And-- and the people-- the people started to run around.
They didn't know what to do.
You know, they came in our town, our city, came from Warsaw-- from all over the country [? was ?] [? came ?] people hiding.
And they think this is a good hiding place.
But the Germans came in.
First-- the first thing that they-- that they bombed right away our city in-- in-- in September the  1st.
Yeah.
The--
And we-- the whole family-- the whole family-- our family went in the forest.
It was not far from our city and was hiding.
And because-- because we knew it was going to be bombing.
And we started to go away there, walk away, and the bombs started to fly.
A few things-- they had broken up the-- the synagogue.
When-- when he started to walk away, the same street in the synagogue was-- was still close.
They smashed up the synagogue right-- right at the first day.
Yeah.
We--
We couldn't [LAUGHS] look back.
They have to run.
So we was there a couple days in the-- in the city-- in the forest.
And then-- then on the second day in the-- in the evening, in the middle of the night, we came to get something from the house.
It was the house.
It was close, not far from the temple.
And the house was smashed all to pieces.
So we started to get in and get something out.
And we went away while it's dark.
Well, we just looking for food.
We-- we put it in the clothes.
We ran away with the best clothes.
Well, just there was trees.
Was-- was laying on the ground a few days.
And then when it's-- when it's everything-- you already quieted down.
The Germans came in.
And we came back the city.
Yeah, yeah.
Oh, we-- we saw them.
They were in the city.
It was-- they were going around coming and going out.
So the army was going to get through.
So in the meantime, they was-- they was there about a week or two in the-- in the city.
And then they started coming in close-- the Russians.
Yeah.
So finally, the-- the Russians came in.
And they moved out the Germans.
And then the Russians were maybe about-- about three or four weeks in the city.
And then-- then we found out as they started the people to talk are the Russians going to go away, because they got a deal with the-- with the Germans just to go to the River Bug.
And it's not far-- the Bug was there.
So when they-- then they started to go back, the Russians, and the Germans came back.
Yeah.
Well, we was-- we was in the city.
We were hanging around the city.
Well, we lived close in that house-- in that house, not far.
The house was not demolished completely.
But we were staying around in the meantime.
Yeah, it was-- was close, that house.
So in the meantime, the-- the-- the Russians started to move out.
So we decided to move out too.
So we took out-- we-- we hired a horse and buggy.
We-- we put it in-- we put it in our-- this-- we got a lot of shoes still.
We took it out from that house and put it in.
And we went over the Bug.
And from there, Siemiatycze-- I don't know if you got it marked down.
And from Siemiatycze, we-- we took a train.
And we went to Lida then Vilnius.
We had a lot of customers in Lida.
And we came there.
And we was-- we was there a nice couple months.
And then the-- the Russians were there.
By train.
Yeah, and we was there on that-- we found a little house.
And we lived there for a couple months.
It was Ukraine.
It was Poland.
It was Ukrainian side.
And then when-- when the Germans was very close, then they attacked Russia.
And they attacked.
And it was close already to Rovno.
So-- so I-- I decided to go to Russia-- walk away.
And my parents.
They were still there-- the whole family.
[DRINKS] My sister-- one sister Chaja, she wants to go with us.
My mother didn't let her.
Kiev.
In Kiev.
They arrested-- the police, but they let us out right away.
No.
[INAUDIBLE] So then we came there.
And he was not there.
He was working night shift.
So his wife recognized me.
She said I looked like her husband.
[LAUGHS] So she knew right who I am.
Anyway, we had to go back to the square.
And we-- we have to come back later.
In a couple hours, she was gonna be back from work.
And finally, I see I went to-- [? and you ?] see, we were stuck there.
So anyway, I saw a table where there was sitting a Jewish-- Jewish officer.
I went to  him.
And I said, I got some packages in the square.
And I want-- I want to get out.
And I wanted my passport.
He said-- he said, he wanted to help us.
He said, I'll give it to you.
But you have to come back.
If you don't come back, you're going to be in big trouble.
And he was there.
And I-- I got a [INAUDIBLE] there.
And they put me into fix the shoes for the people-- thousands of people.
So in-- on one side, it was the biggest [? war there. ?] One one side, it was Germans there-- 5,000, 6,000.
Change?
